museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: The busiest drawer of 1979: Asteroids, Microvision, Atari's 400/800 computers, Video Chess, Heiankyo Alien and late-year Space Invaders/Galaxian trade movement crowd the case.
museum.curatorNote: November has enough anchors for a fuller release shelf. Some items are regional debuts or shipping windows, so the wording stays careful.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: Five researched moments from a year where arcade, home-computer and magazine evidence rarely lines up like a modern launch calendar.

items.title: Asteroids reaches arcades
items.body: Atari's Asteroids is released in November, turning vector minimalism into one of the defining arcade experiences of the coming year.
items.sourceNote: The Strong lists Asteroids as released in November 1979.

items.title: Atari 400 and 800 begin shipping
items.body: After their January showing, Atari's home computers ship in November, bringing custom graphics and sound into the domestic computer conversation.
items.sourceNote: Atari 8-bit sources cite November 1979 shipping.

items.title: Microvision launches the cartridge handheld idea
items.body: Milton Bradley's Microvision arrives as the first cartridge-based handheld system, a fragile but fascinating branch of portable gaming history.
items.sourceNote: Microvision sources cite November 1979; Guinness recognises it as first cartridge-based handheld.

items.title: Intellivision begins limited test-market life
items.body: Mattel's Intellivision appears in limited US test markets, still far from ordinary UK awareness.
items.sourceNote: Atari Archive describes November 1979 Intellivision test launch.

items.title: Atari Video Chess finally appears
items.body: Atari's long-delayed Video Chess reaches the VCS, a prestige cartridge that makes the tiny console perform a difficult board-game trick.
items.sourceNote: Atari Archive cites November 1979 release evidence.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: Eight November-window releases and launch items, with regional caveats kept visible.

items.title: Asteroids
items.body: Atari's vector asteroid field, a minimalist cabinet that became a giant arcade presence.
items.sourceNote: The Strong and MobyGames list November 1979.

items.title: Video Chess
items.body: A delayed but technically impressive Atari chess cartridge, sold as a premium home-console release.
items.sourceNote: Atari Archive cites November 1979 period evidence.

items.title: Block Buster
items.body: The Microvision pack-in/launch game, turning a handheld LCD unit into a cartridge platform rather than a single fixed toy.
items.sourceNote: Microvision sources cite November 1979 release; launch-game specifics should be verified before final publication.

items.title: Super Invader
items.body: A Space Invaders-inspired Apple II game from Creative Computing Software, later remembered as a home-arcade bridge.
items.sourceNote: Super Invader references cite November 1979 publication.

items.title: Lunar Rescue
items.body: Taito's lunar-themed arcade game, arriving in the same year that Lunar Lander made descent and fuel part of arcade play.
items.sourceNote: Lunar Rescue sources list November 1979.

items.title: Heiankyo Alien
items.body: A Japanese maze game whose trap-and-bury play stands apart from simple shooting, with roots in university computer culture.
items.sourceNote: Heiankyo Alien sources cite November 1979 arcade release after earlier computer origin.

items.title: Basketball
items.body: A launch-window Atari 8-bit cartridge, showing how Atari brought sports and console thinking into its new computer line.
items.sourceNote: Atari computer museum references list Basketball publication in November 1979.

items.title: Space Invaders Deluxe / Part II
items.body: Midway debuted the North American version around November before mass production in January 1980.
items.sourceNote: Space Invaders Part II sources cite November 1979 North American debut and January 1980 mass production.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: Four hardware/context objects explaining the month without pretending Britain had a mature games retail shelf yet.

items.title: Milton Bradley Microvision
items.body: The first cartridge-based handheld console: primitive, expensive, and historically startling because it makes portable interchangeable games visible a decade before Game Boy.
items.sourceNote: Microvision sources cite November 1979; Guinness recognises first cartridge-based handheld.

items.title: Atari VCS as the home reference point
items.body: In November 1979 the Atari VCS was not yet the renamed 2600, but it was becoming shorthand for home videogames in North America. In Britain it remained aspirational, expensive, and less ordinary than the arcade.
items.sourceNote: Atari Archive late-1979 context notes Atari sold roughly 600,000 VCS units over 1979 and passed one million installed.

items.title: Arcade cabinets as the premium display
items.body: The most advanced game experiences were still cabinets: dedicated controls, loud sound, glowing marquees and operators choosing what deserved floor space.
items.sourceNote: Used as period arcade context around Space Invaders, Lunar Lander, Galaxian and Asteroids.

items.title: Home computers before the UK bedroom boom
items.body: The Apple II, PET, TRS-80, Nascom and other early machines made games programmable, but they were not yet the cheap British home-micro explosion of 1982.
items.sourceNote: Supported by Practical Computing, PCW, BYTE and Creative Computing context.

sections.title: Magazine Covers
sections.subtitle: In 1979, the magazine was often the map: part catalogue, part classroom, part rumour network.

items.title: Practical Computing
items.headline: British microcomputer shelf culture
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed placeholder for Practical Computing, November 1979.
items.curatorNote: Practical Computing belongs to the 1979 map of play: not always a games magazine, but part of the paper network that taught people what small computers and arcade culture meant.
items.body: Practical Computing belongs to the 1979 map of play: not always a games magazine, but part of the paper network that taught people what small computers and arcade culture meant.
items.certainty: publication context verified; cover image reconstructed placeholder

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: Online play existed at the edge of institutions and networks, but most players lived in an offline culture of paper, coins and local knowledge.

items.title: Online life was mostly not a visitor experience yet
items.body: For most players in November 1979, games were discovered through arcades, shops, magazines, clubs and word of mouth. The web did not exist, and domestic online play was not part of ordinary gaming life.
items.sourceNote: Included to prevent projecting later online habits back onto 1979.

items.title: MUD1 existed, but behind institutional doors
items.body: Roy Trubshaw and Richard Bartle's MUD work at the University of Essex belongs to this era, but it was a university/mainframe culture, not something a high-street player would casually log into at home.
items.sourceNote: MUD1 sources place creation in 1978 and later network significance through Essex connections.

items.title: Printed listings were the practical network
items.body: For home-computer owners, the closest thing to downloading a game was often typing a listing from a magazine, saving it to cassette, and hoping the checksum of human patience held.
items.sourceNote: Based on Creative Computing, BYTE, Practical Computing and similar magazine culture.

items.title: The arcade was a live feed
items.body: Scores, tricks and rumours updated by watching other people play. A good cabinet in a cafe, arcade or seaside amusement hall could teach faster than any printed manual.
items.sourceNote: Period arcade culture context rather than a single dated claim.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: A month from the pre-specialist-games-magazine world: public cabinets, expensive machines and a future still forming.

items.title: The future was in public
items.body: The arcade future was suddenly a field of white vector rocks, spinning ships and pure panic.

items.title: The home version was still an idea
items.body: Home play split three ways: a VCS cartridge, a serious Atari computer, and a strange handheld that felt more like a sci-fi toy than a platform.

items.title: Britain felt it through arcades and magazines
items.body: For Britain, much of this was glimpsed through import news and magazines while the local arcade remained the place where the future actually took your coins.

items.title: Dates were not launch-day culture yet
items.body: The modern habit of exact worldwide release dates does not fit 1979 neatly. Games appeared through trade shows, operator routes, regional shipments, adverts and later memory.

editorNotes: Initial AI-generated research draft for 1979. Sparse months intentionally avoid invented release lists.
Magazine cover images are reconstructed placeholders and should be replaced with verified/licensed scans if used editorially.
Several arcade release months are regional or disputed; sourceNote fields mark uncertainty.
